name: Divide By Zero
description: The product divides a value by zero.
extended_description: This weakness typically occurs when an unexpected value is provided to the product, or if an error occurs that is not properly detected. It frequently occurs in calculations involving physical dimensions such as size, length, width, and height.

mapping.rationale: This CWE entry is at the Base level of abstraction, which is a preferred level of abstraction for mapping to the root causes of vulnerabilities.

mechanism.root_cause_summary: The product divides a value by zero.
typical_preconditions: untrusted or insufficiently validated state reaches sensitive operation
typical_sources: untrusted or insufficiently validated state reaches sensitive operation
negative_nonfix_signals: style refactor without changing the sensitive path

guard_patterns: same logical object and same sink context

recommended_guard_templates: same logical object and same sink context

stage1.goal: Classify patch chunks by how directly they repair the CWE root cause.
must_focus: direct change to vulnerable sink or validation site
changes that alter boundary, lifecycle, policy, or authorization relation
supporting helper or error-propagation logic needed by the main fix
must_avoid: generic logging changes without security semantics
refactors unrelated to the sensitive sink/path

stage2.goal: Construct discriminative root cause, anchor, predicates, and guards.
must_focus: anchor at the sink or dominant validation block
prefer predicates that encode relations, not lone generic tokens
choose function-local or anchor-window scope whenever possible
must_avoid: predicates that rely on generic names alone
anchors in wrappers that lack the core vulnerability mechanism
recommended_queries: same logical object and same sink context

stage3.goal: Verify whether the target tag still exhibits the vulnerable relation or already contains the fix relation.
must_focus: whether the sensitive sink/path still exists
whether the fix relation is locally present in the same code region
whether anchor relocation is required before concluding absence
must_avoid: declaring NOT_AFFECTED from generic token absence alone
using broad token matches as final evidence

bad_examples.summary: The following Java example contains a function to compute an average but does not validate that the input value used as the denominator is not zero. This will...
bad_examples.snippet: public int computeAverageResponseTime (int totalTime, int numRequests) { return totalTime / numRequests; }

bad_examples.summary: The following C/C++ example contains a function that divides two numeric values without verifying that the input value used as the denominator is not zero....
bad_examples.snippet: double divide(double x, double y){ return x/y; }

bad_examples.summary: The following C# example contains a function that divides two numeric values without verifying that the input value used as the denominator is not zero. This...
bad_examples.snippet: int Division(int x, int y){ return (x / y); }

good_examples.summary: The following Java example contains a function to compute an average but does not validate that the input value used as the denominator is not zero. This will...
good_examples.snippet: public int computeAverageResponseTime (int totalTime, int numRequests) throws ArithmeticException { if (numRequests == 0) { System.out.println("Division by zero attempted!"); throw ArithmeticException; } return...

good_examples.summary: The following C/C++ example contains a function that divides two numeric values without verifying that the input value used as the denominator is not zero....
good_examples.snippet: const int DivideByZero = 10; double divide(double x, double y){ if ( 0 == y ){ throw DivideByZero; } return x/y; } ... try{ divide(10, 0); } catch( int i ){ if(i==DivideByZero) { cerr<<"Divide by zero error"; } }

good_examples.summary: The following C# example contains a function that divides two numeric values without verifying that the input value used as the denominator is not zero. This...
good_examples.snippet: int SafeDivision(int x, int y){ try{ return (x / y); } catch (System.DivideByZeroException dbz){ System.Console.WriteLine("Division by zero attempted!"); return 0; } }

observed_examples.description: Invalid size value leads to divide by zero.
Height value of 0 triggers divide by zero.
